Praise (الحمد) is the commendation for a voluntary beautiful act; thus, it contains an indication of the occurrence of an act from him (peace be upon him and his progeny) from which everyone benefits and profits, and thus they praise him for it. For this reason, he (peace be upon him) said in the previous narration of Ubayd ibn Zurarah: "and there is no one but that he will need the intercession of Muhammad on that day." This meaning will be further explained in another plausible way.

As for His saying, the Exalted: "And soon your Lord will give you [that with which] you will be pleased" being the most hopeful verse in the Book of Allah, rather than His saying, the Exalted: "O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair...", although the prohibition of despair is repeatedly mentioned in the Noble Quran, the saying of him (peace be upon him and his progeny) narrating from Abraham (peace be upon him): **وَمَن يَقْنَطُ مِن رَّحْمَةِ رَبِّهِ...** — "And who despairs of his Lord’s mercy except the astray?" [15:56], and His saying, the Exalted, narrating from Jacob (peace be upon him): **إِنَّهُ لَا يَيْأَسُ مِن رَّوْحِ اللَّهِ...** — "Indeed no one despairs of Allah’s comfort except the faithless lot" [12:87], both refer to despair and hopelessness regarding ontological mercy (الرحمه التكوينية), as evidenced by the context.

As for His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ يَا عِبَادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ...** — "Say, [that Allah declares,] 'O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah forgives all sins. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. Turn penitently to your Lord...'" [39:53-54] until the end of the verses, although it is a prohibition of despair regarding legislative mercy (الرحمة التشريعية)—by the context of His saying "committed excesses against their own souls," which suggests that the despair in the verse is despair due to sin—and although the Glorified has generalized forgiveness for all sins without exception, He concluded it with the command to repent, submit, and act by following. Thus, the verse indicates that the servant who has committed excesses against himself should not despair of the comfort of Allah as long as he can choose repentance, submission, and righteous action.

In short, this is a restricted mercy that Allah has commanded His servants to cling to, and the hope for a restricted mercy is not like the hope for a general mercy and an absolute giving and pleasing that Allah promised to His Messenger, whom He made a mercy to all the worlds. That promise gladdens the soul of the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) with His saying, the Exalted: "And soon your Lord will give you [that with which] you will be pleased."

To clarify: the verse is in the context of bestowing favor (الامتنان), and it contains a promise specific to the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny), the like of which Allah the Glorified never promised to any of His creation. He did not restrict the giving to anything; it is an absolute giving. Allah has promised something similar to a group of His servants in Paradise, saying: **لَهُم مَّا يَشَاءُونَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ...** — "They will have whatever they wish with their Lord" [42:22], and: **لَهُم مَّا يَشَاءُونَ فِيهَا وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ...** — "There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more" [50:35]. Thus, He informed that they have there what is beyond their wish. Wishing (المشيَّة) relates to everything that occurs to the human mind of felicity and good; thus, there is that which has not occurred to the heart of any mortal, as He said: **فَلَا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ مَّا أُخْفِيَ لَهُم...** — "No one knows what has been kept in store for them out of the delight of the eyes" [32:17]. If this is the extent of what Allah has given to His servants who believed and did righteous deeds—which is a matter beyond measure, as you have known—then what He gives to His Messenger (peace be upon him and his progeny) in the context of bestowing favor is wider and greater; so understand.

This is the status of His giving, the Exalted. As for the status of the pleasure (رضى) of the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny), it is well known that this pleasure is not the pleasure with what Allah has apportioned, which is a companion to the command of Allah. For Allah is the Absolute Owner and Self-Sufficient, and the servant has nothing but poverty and need; thus, he ought to be pleased with the little and the much that his Lord gives him, and he ought to be pleased with what Allah has decreed for him, whether it pleases or grieves him. If this is so, then the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) is the most knowledgeable and the most active in this; he desires nothing but what Allah desires for him. However, this pleasure, since it is placed in contrast to giving, conveys another meaning, similar to enriching the poor with what he laments the loss of, and pleasing the hungry by satiating him. It is the pleasing through giving without limit. This also is something Allah promised the like of to a group of His servants. He, the most Excellent of speakers, said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ...** — "Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds—it is they who are the best of creatures. Their reward, near their Lord, is the Gardens of Eden, with streams running in them, to remain in them forever. Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him. That is for him who fears his Lord" [98:7-8]. This also, due to its position of bestowing favor and specificity, must be something beyond what the believers have and wider than that. The Exalted has said regarding His Messenger: **بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ...** — "most kind and merciful to the faithful" [9:128]. Thus, He confirmed his kindness. How could the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) be pleased and his soul be at peace while he enjoys the delights of Paradise and strolls in its gardens, while a group of the believers are entangled in the depths of the Blaze, imprisoned under the layers of the Fire, while they acknowledge the Lordship of Allah, the Messengership of His Messenger, and the truth of what he brought? It was only that ignorance overcame them and Satan played with them, so they committed sins without defiance or arrogance. If one of us reviews what has passed of his life and looks at how he fell short in perfection and advancement, he blames himself for his negligence in his striving and seeking; then he considers the ignorance of youth and the lack of experience, and perhaps the fire of his anger is extinguished and the surge of his blame is broken due to a partial mercy that Allah deposited in his primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being*. What then do you think of the mercy of the Lord of all the worlds in a station where there is nothing but the ignorance of a weak human, the dignity of the kind and merciful Prophet, and the mercy of the Most Merciful of the merciful? And he has seen what he has seen of the consequence of his affair from the moment the claws of death seized him until the final stations of the Day of Resurrection.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا تَنفَعُ الشَّفَاعَةُ عِندَهُ إِلَّا لِمَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ...** — "Intercession is of no avail with Him, except for those whom He permits" [34:23]. Narrated from Abu al-Abbas al-Mukabbir, who said: A client (مولى) of a woman of Ali ibn al-Husayn, called Abu Ayman, entered and said: "O Abu Ja'far, you deceive the people and say: 'The intercession of Muhammad, the intercession of Muhammad'!" Abu Ja'far [Imam al-Baqir] became angry until his face changed color, then he said: **ويحك يا أبا أيمن أغرّك أن عفّ بطنك وفرجك...** — "Woe to you, O Abu Ayman! Has it deceived you that your belly and your private parts are chaste? If you were to see the terrors of the Resurrection, you would surely find yourself in need of the intercession of Muhammad. Woe to you! Does he intercede except for those for whom the Fire has become mandatory?" He said: **ما من أحد من الأولين والآخرين إلا وهو محتاج إلى شفاعة محمد...** — "There is no one from the first to the last but that he is in need of the intercession of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) on the Day of Resurrection." Then Abu Ja'far said: **إن لرسول الله الشفاعة في امته...** — "Indeed, the Messenger of Allah has intercession for his community, and we have intercession for our Shi'a, and our Shi'a have intercession for their families." Then he said: **وإن المؤمن ليشفع في مثل ربيعة ومضر...** — "And indeed, the believer shall intercede for the likes of [the tribes of] Rabi'ah and Mudar; and indeed, the believer shall intercede for his servant and say: 'O Lord, the right of my service! He used to protect me from the heat and the cold.'"

**I say:** His saying (peace be upon him): "There is no one from the first to the last but that he is in need of the intercession of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny)"—its apparent meaning is that this general intercession is other than the one he mentioned by saying: "Woe to you! Does he intercede except for those for whom the Fire has become mandatory?" A similar meaning has already been mentioned in the narration of al-Ayyashi from Ubayd ibn Zurarah from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him). Regarding this meaning, there are other narrations narrated by both the general and the specific sources. It is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ الشَّفَاعَةَ...** — "Those whom they invoke besides Him have no power of intercession, excepting such as testify to the truth and they have knowledge" [43:86], as it implies that the criterion for intercession is testimony (الشهادة). Thus, the witnesses (الشهداء) are the intercessors who possess intercession. It will come, Allah willing, in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَذَٰلِكَ جَعَلْنَاكُمْ أُمَّةً وَسَطًا...** — "Thus We have made you a middle nation that you may be witnesses to the people, and that the Apostle may be a witness to you" [2:143], that the Prophets are witnesses and that Muhammad (peace be upon him and his progeny) is a witness over them. Thus, he (peace be upon him and his progeny) is the witness of the witnesses; therefore, he is the intercessor of the intercessors. Were it not for the testimony of the witnesses, the foundation of the Resurrection would not stand.

Also in *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Intercession is of no avail with Him, except for those whom He permits." He (peace be upon him) said: **لا يشفع أحد من أنبياء الله ورسله...** — "None of the Prophets of Allah or His Messengers shall intercede until Allah permits him, except for the Messenger of Allah, for Allah permitted him intercession before the Day of Resurrection. Intercession is for him and for the Imams from his progeny, then after that for the Prophets."

In *al-Khisal*, narrated from Imam ‘Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Three groups shall intercede with Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and their intercession will be accepted: the Prophets, then the scholars, then the martyrs.'"

**I say:** It appears that the intended meaning of "the martyrs" (الشهداء) here refers to those slain on the battlefield of combat, as is commonly understood in the parlance of the Imams in the narrations, rather than the "witnesses of deeds" (شهداء الأعمال) as per the Quranic terminology.

In *al-Khisal*, in the "Hadith of the Four Hundred," he (peace be upon him) said: "We possess the power of intercession, and those who love us possess the power of intercession."

**I say:** There are numerous narrations regarding the intercession of the Mistress of Women, Fatimah (peace be upon her), the intercession of her progeny other than the Imams, and the intercession of the believers—even the miscarried fetus among them. In the well-known hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): **تناكحوا تناسلوا فإني أُباهي بكم الامم يوم القيامة ولو بالسقط يقوم محبنطئاً على باب الجنة فيقال له : أُدخل فيقول : لا حتى يدخل أبواي** — "Marry and procreate, for I shall boast of your numbers before the nations on the Day of Resurrection, even by the miscarried fetus who will stand aggrieved at the gate of Paradise. It will be said to him: 'Enter,' and he will reply: 'No, not until my parents enter,'" to the end of the hadith.

In *al-Khisal*, from Abu ‘Abdillah, from his father, from his grandfather, from ‘Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Paradise has eight gates: a gate through which the Prophets and the truthful (الصديقون) enter; a gate through which the martyrs and the righteous (الصالحون) enter; and five gates through which our Shi‘a and those who love us enter. I shall remain standing upon the Path (الصراط), supplicating and saying: 'My Lord, keep safe my Shi‘a, those who love me, my helpers, and those who took me as a guardian in the worldly abode.' Then a call will come from the depths of the Throne: 'Your supplication has been answered, and your intercession for your Shi‘a has been accepted.' Every man from among my Shi‘a, those who took me as a guardian, helped me, and fought those who showed enmity toward me by deed or word, shall intercede for seventy thousand of his neighbors and relatives. And there is a gate through which the rest of the Muslims enter—those who bear witness that there is no god but Allah and in whose hearts there was not an atom’s weight of hatred toward us, the People of the House (أهل البيت)."

In *al-Kafi*, from Hafs the Mu’adhin, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) in his letter to his companions, he (peace be upon him) said: "Know that none of Allah’s creation can avail you against Him—neither a proximate angel (ملك مقرب) nor a sent messenger (نبي مرسل), nor anyone besides them. Whoever desires that the intercession of the intercessors (شفاعة الشافعين) should benefit him before Allah, let him beseech Allah to be pleased with him."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Furat, with its chain of transmission from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "Jabir said to Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): 'May I be your ransom, O son of the Messenger of Allah! Relate to me a hadith concerning your grandmother Fatimah.'" He then cited the hadith mentioning the intercession of Fatimah on the Day of Resurrection until he said: "Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: 'By Allah, none shall remain among the people [at that stage] except the doubter, the disbeliever, or the hypocrite. When they are placed between the ranks, they will cry out as Allah the Exalted said: **فَمَا لَنَا مِن شَافِعِينَ (100) وَلَا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ (101) فَلَوْ أَنَّ لَنَا كَرَّةً فَنَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (102)** (100. Now we have no intercessors, 101. nor any sympathetic friend. 102. If only there were another turn for us, we would be among the faithful.)' Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: 'Far from it, far from it! They are denied what they seek; **وَلَوْ رُدُّوا لَعَادُوا لِمَا نُهُوا عَنْهُ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكَاذِبُونَ** (Were they sent back, they would surely return to what they were forbidden, and they are indeed liars.)'"

**I say:** His (peace be upon him) reliance upon the saying of the Exalted: **فَمَا لَنَا مِن شَافِعِينَ** (Now we have no intercessors) indicates an intuitive understanding that these verses signify the actual occurrence of intercession. Those who deny intercession have also relied on this verse to negate it. However, it has become clear from what we previously presented regarding the saying of the Exalted: **فَمَا تَنفَعُهُمْ شَفَاعَةُ الشَّافِعِينَ** (So the intercession of the intercessors will not avail them) how it indicates the occurrence of intercession in general. For if the intent were mere negation, the proper phrasing would have been: "Now we have no intercessor (شفيع) nor any sympathetic friend." Thus, using the plural form (شافعين) within the context of negation indicates that intercession does occur from a group, but it does not benefit them. Furthermore, the saying of the Exalted: **فَلَوْ أَنَّ لَنَا كَرَّةً فَنَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** (If only there were another turn for us, we would be among the faithful), coming after the saying: **فَمَا لَنَا مِن شَافِعِينَ وَلَا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ**, is framed as a lamentation (التحسر) and a wish (تمنّ) occurring within that lament. It is well known that a wish in the context of lamentation only concerns that which one has lost and that which one grieves over. Thus, the meaning of their saying: "If only there were another turn for us," is: "O, would that we were returned so that we might be among the believers and thereby attain the intercession of the intercessors just as the believers attained it." Therefore, this verse is among those that prove the occurrence of intercession.

In *al-Tawhid*, from al-Kazim, from his father, from his forefathers, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: "My intercession is only for those of my nation who have committed major sins (أهل الكبائر); as for the virtuous (المحسنون), there is no way against them." It was asked: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, how can there be intercession for those who commit major sins when Allah the Exalted says: **وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ** (and they do not intercede except for him with whom He is pleased), and one who commits a major sin is not one with whom He is pleased?" He (peace be upon him) replied: "There is no believer who commits a sin except that it distresses him and he regrets it. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Regret suffices as repentance (توبة).' And he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Whoever is gladdened by his good deed and distressed by his evil deed is a believer.' Thus, whoever does not regret a sin he commits is not a believer, and intercession is not mandatory for him; he is a wrongdoer (ظالم), and Allah, exalted be His mention, says: **مَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ يُطَاعُ** (The wrongdoers will have no sympathetic friend, nor any intercessor who would be heard.)" It was said to him: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, how can one who does not regret a sin he commits not be a believer?" He replied: "No one commits a major sin among acts of disobedience while knowing he will be punished for it except that he regrets what he committed. Whenever he regrets, he is a repentant one (تائب) deserving of intercession. But whenever he does not regret it, he is persistent (مصر), and the persistent one is not forgiven because he does not believe in the punishment for what he committed. Had he believed in the punishment, he would have regretted it. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'No major sin remains with seeking forgiveness (استغفار), and no minor sin remains with persistence (إصرار).' As for the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ**, they do not intercede except for him whose religion (دين) Allah is pleased with. Religion is the affirmation of recompense for good and evil deeds. Thus, whoever’s religion is pleasing will regret the sins he committed due to his knowledge of their consequence on the Day of Resurrection."

**I say:** His (peace be upon him) saying: "and he was a wrongdoer (ظالماً)," contains a definition of the "wrongdoer" on the Day of Resurrection and an allusion to how the Quran defines him where it says: **فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن لَّعْنَةُ اللَّهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ (44) الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوجًا وَهُم بِالْآخِرَةِ كَافِرُونَ (45)** (44. ...Thereupon a herald will announce among them: ‘May Allah’s curse be on the wrongdoers! 45. Those who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked, and who disbelieve in the Hereafter.’) This refers to one who does not believe in the Day of Recompense; thus, he does not grieve over missing Allah’s commands, nor is he distressed by violating His prohibitions—either by denying all true knowledge and religious teachings or by belittling them and disregarding the recompense and the religion on the Day of Recompense. In such a case, his verbal profession of it would be a mockery of its command and a denial of it. His (peace be upon him) saying: "then you are a repentant one deserving of intercession," means one who has returned to Allah, possessing a pleasing religion (دين مرضي) and thus deserving of intercession. As for "repentance" (التوبة) in its technical sense, it is itself an intercessor and a means of salvation. His (peace be upon him) saying: "The Prophet said: 'No major sin remains with seeking forgiveness...'"—his reliance on this is because persistence (الإصرار), which is the lack of aversion to the sin and the absence of regret for it, removes the sin from its normal status to another status, which is the denial of the Return (المعاد) and wrongdoing (الظلم) against the signs of Allah. Thus, it is not forgiven, for a sin is only forgiven either through repentance or through intercession, both of which are contingent upon a pleasing religion; and in that case, there is neither repentance nor a pleasing religion.

A similar meaning occurs in a narration in *al-‘Ilal* from Abu Ishaq al-Laythi, who said: "I said to Abu Ja‘far Muhammad ibn ‘Ali al-Baqir (peace be upon him): 'O son of the Messenger of Allah, inform me about the insightful believer (المؤمن المستبصر): when he reaches the peak of knowledge and becomes perfect, does he commit adultery?' He said: 'By Allah, no.' I said: 'Does he commit sodomy?' He said: 'By Allah, no.' I said: 'Does he steal?' He said: 'No.' I said: 'Does he drink wine?' He said: 'No.' I said: 'Does he commit any of these major sins or any of these indecencies?' He said: 'No.' I said: 'Does he commit any sin at all?' He said: 'Yes, and he is a sinning believer who is a *Muslim*.' I said: 'What is the meaning of *Muslim*?' He said: 'A *Muslim* is one who does not let the sin cling to him nor persists in it,'" to the end of the hadith.

In *al-Khisal*, with chains of transmission from al-Rida, from his forefathers (peace be upon them), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'When the Day of Resurrection comes, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, will manifest Himself to His believing servant and confront him with his sins, one by one. Then Allah will forgive him; Allah will not let a proximate angel or a sent messenger see them, and He will conceal them so that no one discovers them. Then He will say to his evil deeds: "Become good deeds."'"

From *Sahih Muslim*, attributed to Abu Dharr, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'A man will be brought on the Day of Resurrection and it will be said: "Present his minor sins to him and set aside his major ones." It will be said: "You did such and such on such and such a day," and he will confess, not denying it, while he is terrified of the major sins. Then it will be said: "Give him a good deed in place of every evil deed." He will then say: "Indeed, I have sins that I do not see here."' He [Abu Dharr] said: 'I saw the Messenger of Allah laugh until his premolars were visible.'"

In *al-Amali*, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "When the Day of Resurrection comes, Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, will spread His mercy such that even Iblis will crave His mercy."

**I say:** The last three narrations are among the general reports (المطلقات). The reports indicating the occurrence of the intercession of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) on the Day of Resurrection—from the paths of the Imams of the People of the House as well as from the paths of the *Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama‘ah*—reach the level of mass-transmission (التواتر). Taken as a whole, they indicate a single meaning: intercession for the sinners among the people of faith, either by preventing them from entering the Fire or by taking them out of it after they have entered. What is certain from them is that the sinners among the people of faith will not abide eternally in the Fire. You have already seen that the Quran also does not indicate more than that.

## (Philosophical Discussion)

Although rational proofs (البراهين العقلية) fall short of providing the specific details mentioned in the Book and the Sunnah regarding the Return (المعاد)—due to their inability to grasp the intermediate premises (المقدمات المتوسطة) required for such deduction, as noted by Shaykh Ibn Sina—they do grasp the intellectual (العقلية) and imaginal (المثالية) perfections that man will face on the paths of felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة) after the soul separates from the body, based on the rational and imaginal immateriality (التجرد) for which proof has been established.

In his initial state, every action a human performs produces a psychic disposition (هيئة نفسانية) and a state (حال) of felicity or wretchedness. By felicity, we mean what is good for him insofar as he is human, and by wretchedness, its opposite. Through repetition, these states become a deeply rooted habit (ملكة), from which an "image" (صورة)—either felicitous or wretched—is formed for the soul, serving as the principle for further psychic dispositions and images. If it is felicitous, its effects are existential (وجودية) and compatible with the new image and with the soul, which acts as the receptive matter (المادة القابلة) for it. If it is wretched, its effects are privative (عدمية) matters that, upon analysis, trace back to loss and evil. The felicitous soul finds pleasure in its effects insofar as it is human, and insofar as it is an actually felicitous human. As for the wretched soul, although its effects are familiar and compatible with it insofar as it is their principle, it suffers pain through them insofar as it is human. This applies to souls that are complete in their felicity or wretchedness—meaning the human who is felicitous in essence (ذاتاً) and righteous in action, and the human who is wretched in essence and wicked in action.

As for those deficient in their felicity or wretchedness—meaning the human who is felicitous in essence but wretched in action—this refers to one whose essence possesses a felicitous image through true and firm belief, yet his soul contains wretched and vile dispositions from sins and offenses acquired during its attachment to the worldly body and its nursing from the breast of choice. These are forced (قسرية) matters, incompatible with his essence. Proof has been established that what is forced does not endure (القسر لا يدوم). Thus, this soul will be granted purification from them in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — or on the Day of Resurrection, depending on the strength of their rooting in the soul. The same applies to the accidental felicitous dispositions of a wretched soul; they will be stripped away and vanish, whether quickly or slowly. As for the soul whose actuality (فعلية) of felicity or wretchedness was not completed in the worldly life such that it departed the body in a state of weakness and deficiency (مستضعفة ناقصة), it is among those deferred to the command of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. This is what rational proofs necessitate regarding recompense through reward and punishment, necessitating that they be among the concomitants and results of actions; for the conventional and relative (الوضعية الإعتبارية) links must ultimately trace back in the Hereafter to real, existential (حقيقية وجودية) links.

Furthermore, proofs are established that existential perfection (الكمال الوجودي) varies according to the levels of perfection, deficiency, intensity, and weakness—this is systematic ambiguity (التشكيك), particularly in immaterial light. Thus, these souls have different ranks in proximity to and distance from the Principle of perfection and its ultimate end in their upward journey and return to that from which they began; they are ranked one above the other. This is the nature of efficient causes (العلل الفاعلية) — *in the sense of "that by which" (ما به)* — and the intermediaries of grace (وسائط الفيض). Some souls—namely the complete and perfect souls like those of the Prophets (peace be upon them), especially those at the highest degrees of perfection and actuality—act as intermediaries in the removal of forced, wretched, and vile dispositions from the souls of the weak and those below them among the felicitous when such dispositions have forcibly clung to them. This is the intercession (الشفاعة) specific to those who have committed sins.

## (Social Discussion)

The principles of sociology dictate that human society cannot preserve its life or sustain its existence except through established and recognized laws (قوانين موضوعة معتبرة) that oversee its condition and govern the actions and affairs of individuals. These laws arise from the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — of society and the instinct of the individuals within it, according to existing conditions. Guided by these laws, all social classes move forward, each according to what suits its status and fits its position. Through this, society moves at a swift pace, and from the harmony of its parts and the interaction of its diverse elements, social justice (العدل الاجتماعي) is born. These laws are established for material interests and benefits required for the advancement of material society, as well as for spiritual perfections like noble and virtuous ethics—such as truthfulness in speech, fulfillment of covenants, sincerity, and so on—to which social well-being calls.

Since laws and rulings are conventional (وضعية) rather than ontological (حقيقية), they require the completion of their effect through the establishment of other prescribed rulings regarding recompense (المجازاة), to serve as a safeguard for their sanctity against the transgression of impulsive individuals and the negligence of others. Therefore, whenever a government—any government—is strong in enforcing the regulations of recompense, society does not falter in its progress, nor does the traveler stray from his path and goal. But whenever it weakens, chaos and disorder (الهرج والمرج) intensify within it, and it deviates from its course. Among the necessary teachings to be solidified in society is the indoctrination of the matter of recompense and the creation of belief in it within the souls of individuals. It is mandatory to guard against the entry of hope into their souls for escaping the judgment of recompense and the consequences of opposition and disobedience through intercession, bribery, or any of the destructive ruses and intrigues.

For this reason, some have criticized the Christian religion for what occurred within it—that Christ ransomed the people for their sins through his crucifixion. Thus, people rely on him to save them from the hand of judgment on the Day of Resurrection. In such a case, religion would be a destroyer of humanity, a delayer of civilization, and a force that drives man backward, as has been said. Statistics are cited to show that religious people are more prone to lying and further from justice than others, and this is only because they rely on the truth of their religion and the storage of intercession for their benefit on the Day of Resurrection; thus, they do not care what they do. This is in contrast to others, who were left to their instincts and primordial natures, the judgment of which was not nullified by what nullified it in the religious. Thus, their nature judged the ugliness of deviating from what contradicts the judgment of humanity and virtuous civilization.

Based on this, a group of researchers has resorted to interpreting what has been narrated specifically regarding intercession in Islam, which the Book has spoken of and the Sunnah has mass-transmitted.

By my life, neither does Islam establish intercession in the sense they have interpreted, nor does the intercession it establishes produce the effect they have imagined for it. It is mandatory for the researcher in religious knowledge—and in applying what Islam has legislated to the structure of a sound society and virtuous civilization—to grasp the entirety of what Islam intended of principles and laws applicable to society, and the manner of that application. Then he should grasp what the promised intercession is and what its place and position are among the teachings it brought.

**He will know, firstly:** that the intercession the Quran establishes is that the believers will not abide eternally in the Fire on the Day of Resurrection, provided they meet their Lord with a pleasing faith (الإيمان المرضي) and the true religion (الدين الحق). Thus, it is a promise the Quran has made conditionally. Then it stated that faith, in terms of its persistence, is in grave danger due to sins, especially major ones, and particularly addiction to them and persistence in them; for that is the brink of a precipice of permanent destruction. Through this, the hope of salvation and the fear of destruction are attained, and the soul of the believer moves between fear and hope (الخوف والرجاء). Thus, he worships his Lord out of desire and awe, and moves through his life in a balanced course, deviating neither toward the lethargy of despair nor the laziness of overconfidence.

**Secondly:** Islam has established social laws—both material and spiritual—that encompass all individual and social movements and stillness. Then, for every article of its laws, it considered the appropriate consequence and recompense, ranging from blood money (دية), prescribed punishments (حد), and discretionary punishments (تعزير), until it reaches the deprivation of social privileges, blame, censure, and condemnation. Then it safeguarded this after empowering the government of the guardians of the affair (أولياء الأمر) by giving everyone authority over everyone through "enjoining the good and forbidding the evil" (الأمر بالمعروف والنهي عن المنكر). Then it brought this to life by breathing into it the spirit of religious call, which includes warning and glad tidings of punishment and reward in the Hereafter. It built the foundation of its education by indoctrinating the knowledge of the Beginning and the Return (المبدء والمعاد) upon this order.

This is what Islam aims for in its teaching; the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) brought it, and the experiments that occurred in his era and the era of those who followed him confirmed it, until the hands of the rulers in the Umayyad dynasty and those who followed them toyed with it in their despotism and their playing with the rulings of religion and their nullification of religious limits and policies. This continued until matters reached what they have reached today: the banners of "freedom" were raised, Western civilization appeared, and nothing remained of religion among Muslims except like the dregs in a vessel. This manifest weakness in religious policy and the regression of Muslims is the cause of their decline in virtues and excellences, their degradation in noble ethics and manners, their immersion in amusements and desires, and their plunging into indecencies and abominations. It is this that drove them to violate every sanctity and commit everything that even those not affiliated with religion find repulsive—not what the objector imagines of attributing corruption to certain religious teachings that have no goal or content except the felicity of man in his future and his present. And Allah is the Helper. The statistics they mentioned were only conducted on a collective of "religious" people who have no guardian or strong protector, and on a collective of those not affiliated with religion whose social education and training act as guardians and protectors of their social well-being. Thus, such statistics do not prove anything regarding what he intended.
